questions.question: Explain the key differences between CPU and GPU architecture in terms of optimization goals and core design.
keyPoints: CPU optimizes for latency with complex control logic and large caches
GPU optimizes for throughput with thousands of simple cores
CPU has few cores (2-16) executing complex instructions
GPU has thousands of simple cores executing simple operations in parallel

questions.question: What is Amdahl's Law? Explain why it limits the maximum speedup in parallel computing.
keyPoints: Amdahl's Law: Speedup = 1/(S + P/N)
S is sequential fraction, P is parallel fraction, N is number of cores
Sequential portion sets upper bound on achievable speedup
Even with infinite cores, maximum speedup limited by 1/S

questions.question: Describe the concept of 'latency hiding' in GPU architecture with an example.
keyPoints: GPUs hide memory latency by running many concurrent warps
When some warps wait for memory, others execute
Example: 1000 warps running, 900 waiting for memory, 100 active - no idle cycles
Requires large number of threads to achieve effective hiding

questions.question: What is SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Threads) execution model used in GPUs?
keyPoints: SIMT combines SIMD with multithreading
Threads execute same instruction but on different data
Threads can branch independently (with performance cost)
Warp = group of 32 threads executing in lockstep

questions.question: Explain the different levels of memory hierarchy in GPUs with their access characteristics.
keyPoints: Registers: fastest, per-thread
Shared memory: fast, per-block, on-chip (16-48KB)
Global memory: slow, off-chip, high bandwidth
Constant/Texture cache: read-only, cached access

questions.question: What is thread divergence in GPUs and why does it affect performance?
keyPoints: When threads in same warp take different execution paths
GPU must execute both paths sequentially, masking inactive threads
Causes serialization of execution within warp
Should be minimized through careful algorithm design

questions.question: Explain the concept of heterogeneous computing and its advantages.
keyPoints: Uses combination of CPU and GPU for different workloads
CPU handles sequential/control-intensive tasks
GPU handles parallel/data-intensive tasks
Maximizes efficiency by using right processor for right task

questions.question: Compare and contrast multicore processors and GPUs in terms of architecture, programming models, and suitable applications. Give examples of when each would be preferred.
keyPoints: Architecture: CPU has few complex cores with large caches; GPU has thousands of simple cores
Memory: CPU has uniform memory; GPU has complex memory hierarchy
Programming: CPU uses threads; GPU uses thousands of threads in warps/blocks
CPU suitable for: sequential tasks, complex control flow, low-latency needs
GPU suitable for: data-parallel operations, SIMD workloads, throughput computing
Examples: CPU for OS, databases; GPU for graphics, ML training, scientific simulations

questions.question: Design a parallel algorithm to add two large arrays of size N using CUDA. Explain the kernel design, thread organization, and calculate the time complexity. Also discuss potential bottlenecks.
keyPoints: Each thread handles one element: index = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x
Thread blocks organize threads (e.g., 256 threads per block)
Global memory access patterns affect performance
Kernel: arrC[i] = arrA[i] + arrB[i]
Time complexity: O(N/P) where P = number of threads
Bottlenecks: memory coalescing, bank conflicts in shared memory, branch divergence
Optimization: use appropriate block size, coalesced memory access

questions.question: Explain CUDA programming model with concepts of kernels, threads, blocks, and grids. How does this model enable parallel execution on GPUs?
keyPoints: Kernel: function executed in parallel by GPU threads
Grid: collection of thread blocks executing kernel
Block: group of up to 1024 threads, executes on one SM
Thread: smallest execution unit, has unique ID
Threads in block can synchronize and share shared memory
Blocks can execute in any order, enabling scalability
Hardware maps blocks to SMs, schedules warps within SMs

questions.question: What is the importance of occupancy in GPU programming? How can you calculate and improve occupancy? Discuss factors that limit occupancy.
keyPoints: Occupancy = active warps / max warps per SM
Higher occupancy generally means better GPU utilization
Factors limiting occupancy: block size, shared memory per block, registers per thread
Calculate: occupancy = (threads/block * blocks/SM) / max_threads/SM
Improving: reduce shared memory usage, minimize register usage, optimize block size
Trade-off: high occupancy not always best if registers/sharing increased
